title: Corbin and the Divine Double
essence: Vervaeke turns to Henry Corbin as the thinker who develops Heidegger's gnosis through the specific resources of Persian Sufism and Neoplatonism. Beginning with Heidegger's commentary on the rose poem of Angelus Silesius, Vervaeke draws the contrast between narrative teleology and vertical ontology: the rose blooms without why, grounding itself in itself, and this non-teleological being mode is what physis means. This is exapted from narrative into a vertical movement from the shallower frame of the current self toward the depths of being. Corbin's central contribution is the distinction between the imaginary (subjective mental imagery, merely possessed) and the imaginal: the transjective use of image that mediates between the abstract intelligible world and the concrete sensible world, and between subjective and objective — in an ongoing transformative transframing. The symbol in Corbin's sense is translucent, transjective, trajective, transformative, and transtemporal. Fundamentalism and literalism destroy the imaginal by collapsing it into either the abstract or the concrete, ending gnosis. The divine double is then introduced: the motif prevalent across Hellenistic Mediterranean spirituality (Stang's scholarship) of a superlative self to which one's current self is bound and toward which one aspires. The divine double as imaginal symbol allows one to internalize a more encompassing frame into one's current frame (the shining in) while being drawn toward the indwelling of that larger world (the withdrawing). LA Paul's transformative experience and Callard's aspiration are brought in as the analytic philosophy of the same process.
keyClaims: Corbin distinguishes the imaginary (subjective mental imagery) from the imaginal: the transjective use of image that mediates between abstract intelligible and concrete sensible worlds in an ongoing transformative transframing.
Fundamentalism and literalism are pernicious precisely because they collapse the imaginal into either the abstract or the concrete, destroying the capacity for gnosis.

The divine double is the Hellenistic-Mediterranean motif of a superlative self to which one is bound and toward which one aspires — transgressive of the romantic grammar that treats the true self as something possessed.
The symbol in Corbin's sense is simultaneously translucent, transjective, trajective, transformative, and transtemporal — the cognitive medium through which gnosis operates.
Corbin connects Heidegger's attunement to gnosis by showing that the imaginal is the mode through which one remembers being in aletheia — recovering the being mode from forgetfulness.

concepts.summary: Corbin's core distinction: the imaginal vs. the imaginary. The imaginal mediates between abstract intelligible and concrete sensible worlds transjectively, in ongoing transformative transframing.
The Hellenistic Mediterranean motif (via Stang's scholarship) of a superlative self toward which one aspires; Corbin's angel is his symbol for the divine double in Persian Sufism.
Corbin develops Heidegger's aletheia into explicit gnosis: the imaginal is the medium through which salvic, transformative participatory knowing operates.
Corbin's symbol is analyzed into five properties: translucent, transjective, trajective, transformative, and transtemporal — all required for the symbol to trigger transframing.
The imaginal's ongoing transformative transframing is the cognitive-scientific correlate of what Corbin calls gnosis through the imaginal symbol.

Callard's aspiration is brought in as the analytic philosophical account of the same process the divine double mythos captures: S1 participates in the emergence of S2 without making or merely receiving it.
LA Paul's account is invoked to explain why the divine double cannot be reasoned toward: the S2 self has perspectival and participatory knowing that S1 cannot access in advance.
Neoplatonism is central to Corbin's formation: his Persian Sufism is deeply shaped by Neoplatonic thought, especially Plotinus, which Corbin reads through Heidegger.
Fundamentalism and literalism are identified as expressions of the having mode: they treat the imaginal as something possessed (imaginary) rather than engaged in becoming (imaginal).

people.summary: The primary subject: his distinction between imaginary and imaginal, his use of Persian Sufism, his development of Heidegger into gnosis, and his notion of the angel as divine double are all examined.
Heidegger's commentary on Angelus Silesius's rose poem, which itself interprets Eckhart, opens the episode; Eckhart's 'live without why' is the non-teleological being mode Corbin builds on.
Heidegger's physis, aletheia, and critique of ontotheology are the backdrop from which Corbin departs; Corbin was one of the first to translate Heidegger into French.
Her transformative experience is used to explain the irreducibility of the divine double aspiration to deliberation — one cannot reason one's way to S2 from S1.
Her proleptic rationality and aspiration account is brought in as the analytic philosophical triangulation of the divine double process.
Neoplatonism's influence on Persian Sufism, and thus on Corbin, runs through Plotinus; Stang identifies the divine double motif also in Plotinus.

crossRefs.context: Heidegger's aletheia, attunement, and gnosis are the foundations on which Corbin builds; this episode develops those into the imaginal and divine double.
The divine double and aspiration are further developed in connection with Jung's individuation and the sacred second self.
